comments.author: @JoshuaDiGiacoma
comments.text:
if you all can, you might want to try to get your CDL and be a truck driver -especially those of you who don't have any/much debt or bills. there are even 1 or more companies out there that will train you themselves  to get the CDL, and 1 or more pay you. But if you go that route be prepared to sign a contract to drive for that company for 6-24 months, and/or repay training fees bit by bit as you work and get paid by said company.

you could maybe get rid of that apartment. and the car if it isn't already paid off. live on the truck as much as you can. experiment and see how long at one time  you can withstand being on the truck. figure out where you can go shopping as a truck driver or base your hometime around how much food you can keep on the truck. use a rental car if you can get one on your time away from the truck. 

you might want to stick with trucking (as shitty as it might be) for 2,3,5 or 10 years because the money may or may not be great as a rookie. assuming it gets better, that's all the more reason to stick with it for awhile. get your endorsements, get experience, and look for better paying trucking jobs as you progress.

but at least you can maybe drop the car and those rent payments. but be sure to be frugal financially on the road as well.save away your money.  keep the wheels turning, because when you're paid by the mile, it becomes "if the wheels ain't turning, you likely ain't earning" Keep your records clean, and follow the rules and laws. Protect that CDL - whether you're on the truck or the car.

comments.author: @griffinkelly8694
comments.text: I'm a writer as well, so I'll start off by saying I feel for the guy. It sucks to lose opportunities and money to a machine. I do also want to know what the content was of these blogs, though. If you're pitching original ideas, finding unique angles, and including exclusive quotes from sources authoritative sources, you will not be replaced by AI as a writer so long as the publication/site wants to maintain that quality of product, at least for now. Who knows, there might be an AI in the future that can do all that. But as for the here and now, I think it's important to know what the content was that he was writing. If it's not what I mentioned before and is instead listicles or essentially PR copy you can find on almost any other blog, then I get how the AI replaced him. Again, I feel for the guy. He said this made up a nice chunk of his income, so it must suck to lose that.

comments.author: @khrystynadesia5160
comments.text: In that situation, you could propose generating more articles with AI, saving your time while keeping the same level of income. For example, if you did 3-4 articles a month, you could propose doing 3 articles a week but using AI (which would take the same time from you) if you wanted to keep the job. The trick is that writing mainly with AI can be boring for many writers, so they quit such jobs and lose money. However, if income is important, people are willing to work, and they can work as AI writers because, anyway, the clients won't lose their time sitting and generating content by themselves. Clients still need a person to write, but with AI to deliver much more content. So, the other side of this situation is that you can become a writer, but with another instrument.

comments.author: @mattlucas992
comments.text: I think soon human creativity will become a premium, whether it's art or just human written blogposts. Automation has decimated human-made industries for decades. eg: shoes, cookware, clothes and any other commodity are no longer made in a small town by a family generational business, they're produced in factories on mass, with mostly machines and few humans overseeing, and the items that are still hand made are now expensive, treasured and luxurious, the fact that they're hand made by people and not manufactured by machines is a bonus to a lot of people. But having said that, who are we to now say that automation is bad because it effects the arts instead of manual labour? Was it bad then too, or just bad now? "AI Is bad!" seems like the rallying cry of a class division between those who sit and create with their mind and those who create with their hands - some people think the former is superior to the latter. "AI" is here to stay, and a lot of people don't like that. My suggestion would be to adapt, and to create on platforms that publicly promote that they do not use AI and everything you're reading is written by an actual person with thoughts and feelings on the matter. Good luck.

In the depths where shadows coil,

Demons hiss at the human soul—

That restless spark, that sacred fire

To shape the world with hand and lyre.


They see the painter’s trembling hand,

The poet’s words like grains of sand,

The music rising, pure and bright—

Each note a star that wounds their night.


In secret councils they conspire,

To forge cold tools of rust and wire,

Machines that hum with hollow breath,

Designed to bleed the arts to death.


They twist the worth of brush and pen,

Reduce the master’s work to “ten,”

Mock every canvas, crush each song,

Until the masses sing along.


For every stroke and crafted tale

Reminds them of their ancient jail,

The Creator’s image stamped in clay

That no dark force can wash away.


So they build and grind and sneer and jeer,

To choke the dream, to kill the seer—

But still one spark will slip the chain,

And again consumed by flame…as demons cheer in their pain.

If there is a universal basic income, this income level can eventually become the new poverty line because people have different lives and therefore different consumer habits. Companies and authorities can also use that as a reason to increase prices especially on utilities. I won't be surprised if most of the basic income money will eventually enter the pocket of the rich monopolies.

"The problem is the reliance on having a job to exist in society" - I totally agree with this. Everything cost money - food, water, housing, retirement, medical treatments... 

- AI can greatly increase the financial independance of those who cannot work (e.g. severely paralysed) working by proxy for them but because there's not much money to be made there compered to other areas I doubt there'd be much progress in that area.

- If AI eventually gains a physical body, getting AI to work by proxy earning money for people can be an idea, but that'd open a new cans of worms such as who would pay for the making, delivery, maintenance and development of these robots.

- I've heard local ministers from my country getting people to all study coding, that AI will open new industries (e.g. maintaining AI) but did not discuss what would happen when AI reaches a stage where it could develop and maintain itself.

- Ultimately, what will happen when AI eventually gains enough sentience and start demanding rights. I know whis sounds kinda ridiculous, but at the rate that AI is developing (developers are just developing them without thinking through the consequences apart from money), it could happen.

Hope you enjoyed the video! Here is the writing prompt I used in the video.

⬇ Prompt used in the video ⬇
When writing, follow these writing guidelines:

- Don't blab.
- Keep sentences brief and clear. Limit to 10-20 words.
- Use everyday words that are easy to understand.
- Pick common words over complex ones. Use technical terms only when needed.
- Avoid words with 4+ syllables. If you must use them, keep surrounding text simple.
- Write for a 8th grade reading level.
- Skip overused business terms and jargon like: delve, digital age, cutting-edge, leverage, proactive, pivotal, seamless, fast-paced, game-changer, quest, realm, landscape, evolve, resilient, thrill, unravel, embark, world.
- Make direct statements without hedging.
- Connect ideas naturally without forced transitions.
- Use standard punctuation.
- Vary sentence structure and punctuation naturally.
- Never use: indeed, furthermore, thus, moreover, notwithstanding, ostensibly, consequently, specifically, notably, alternatively.

I've been running a copywriting agency for ~5 years now and saw this play out in real time - there's always gonna be arbitrage between people who know how to recognise good copy and people who don't.

I'm living that arbitrage right now. My team switched from hiring Upwork copywriters to using Copywhiz AI for about 80% of our client work, and honestly, the AI output converts better for routine stuff. But the thing is - we still need humans with expertise to evaluate what's good and to handle the high-concept creative work.

Hormozi's point about competing against other humans, not AI, resonates hard. We're all using similar tools, but some of us wield them more effectively. The winners aren't the ones who refuse to use AI - they're the ones who leverage it while bringing human judgment and strategic thinking.

Thanks for the response @seanferres  ... I hear ya, but I think it's important to take the next step of game-playing this out. For example, if AI can prepare a meal for you or knit you a sweater, what are all of the supporting aspects of this? What does farming the tomatoes and onions for the salad look like? How do they get gathered, put into warehouses, delivered to a meal-creation center, sliced and diced, etc. Who plans the buildings where they are stored or how the logistics works? What happens when a machine breaks down? What AI does the maintenance and the repairs? Who plans all of this? Who interconnects all of this?

In other words, do we really believe all of this can go on auto-pilot? It's a utopian fantasy IMO. And what about humans and their tendency to never agree on anything? Do we really believe we'll all come together in concert to create this Utopia?

I just don't see it and I love all of the potential AI.  😃

So, you can use the report to inform the Custom Instructions for each session, but you’ll need to re-enter or refer to these guidelines in each new conversation.
